macro_segments.title: Review of Previous Shiur: Bathing, Immersion, and Clothing on Shabbat and Yom Tov

micro_segments.title: Hot water bathing restrictions on Shabbat vs. Yom Tov
Full immersion practices: Ashkenazi vs. Sefardi customs
Removing dirt and stains from garments: embedded vs. surface dirt

macro_segments.title: Restoring Broken Bones and Dislocated Limbs on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: The mishna's prohibition and Shmuel's lenient ruling on bone restoration
Rabbah bar bar Chana forced to study with Rav Yehuda and learning Shmuel's teaching
Dislocated limbs vs. broken bones: different halakhic categories
The gap between Gemara's strictness and practical leniency for sick individuals

macro_segments.title: Borrowing and Lending on Shabbat and Yom Tov: Business Concerns and Writing

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: borrowing wine, oil, and bread with proper language restrictions
Holding collateral (tallis) to ensure repayment after Shabbat
Distinction between she'ilah (borrowing an object) and halva'ah (borrowing fungibles)
Using specific language (hashileni vs. halveini) to avoid writing concerns
Gemara's challenge: imprecise language use and the concern of writing
Creating a distinctive context for Shabbat borrowing through terminology

macro_segments.title: Yom Tov Labor-Saving and Weekday-like Activities (Uvdin d'Chol)

micro_segments.title: Principle of performing Yom Tov activities with a shinuy (variation) when possible
Women filling water buckets: why no shinuy is possible or required
Competing values: uvdin d'chol vs. tircha (effort) in halakhic analysis
Tosafot's view: minimizing weekday-like activities outweighs effort concerns
Application to modern labor-saving devices in the kitchen

macro_segments.title: Rabbinic Discretion: When to Enforce Halachah and When to Remain Silent

micro_segments.title: Clapping and dancing on Yom Tov: concern of making musical instruments
Principle: better people sin in error than willfully when correction is ineffective
Dispute whether this applies equally to Torah violations and rabbinic prohibitions
Example of Yom Kippur eating: not speaking up against known violations
Qualifications by Rishonim on when silence is appropriate vs. when protest is required

macro_segments.title: Clapping and Dancing on Yom Tov: Intrinsic Problem vs. Concern of Consequences

micro_segments.title: Tosefta framing clapping as intrinsic uvdin d'chol; Bavli reframes as concern of making instruments
Tosafot's rationalization: modern practice and whether the gezeirah still applies
Connection to uvdin d'chol discussion and framing concerns as consequences

macro_segments.title: Lending Bread and the Prohibition of Ribis (Interest): Peiros B'Peiros

micro_segments.title: Hillel's ruling: setting monetary value to avoid interest concerns
Gemara's resolution: fixed prices eliminate ribis concern
Practical implications and leniency based on market price stability

macro_segments.title: Yom Tov Loans and Court Enforcement: Dispute Between Rav Yosef and Rava

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef: Yom Tov borrowing is based on trust, not enforceable in court
Rava: loans are enforceable to enable simchat Yom Tov participation
Mishnah's support for enforcement: collateral and post-Yom Tov settlement

macro_segments.title: Shemittah Annulment and Yom Tov Loans: When Debt Can Be Collected

micro_segments.title: Butcher distributing meat on Rosh Hashanah: Shemittah annuls if first day is 30th Elul
Distinction between enforceability and voluntary repayment of Yom Tov loans
Mishnah on Shemittah: lender must verbally annul debt before accepting payment
Rav Avya's scheme: taking collateral to ensure payment after Yom Tov

macro_segments.title: Erev Pesach Sacrifices: Sanctifying Korban Pesach on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: Rav Yochanan: korbanot with fixed times can be sanctified on Shabbat/Yom Tov when needed
Mishnah Erev Pesach example: getting sheep and working out payment later
Gemara's attempt to read cases as pre-sanctified by the seller
Baraita of Rav Hoshaia: explicit sanctification by buyer on Shabbat for Pesach
General principle: sanctification permitted for time-bound obligations only
